content:
**k-means** traces to Stuart Lloyd's work at Bell Labs in 1957 (published 1982) and James MacQueen's independent 1967 formulation—both proposing iterative partitioning by minimizing within-group variance.

The method became a workhorse of **vector quantization** in signal processing and **cluster analysis** in statistics as cheap computation made repeated centroid updates practical on large datasets.

**k-means++** (Arthur and Vassilvitskii, 2007) improved initialization by spreading seeds apart, giving provable approximation guarantees and better empirical stability—now the default in major libraries.

Today k-means remains the default introductory clustering algorithm in data science courses and production EDA pipelines, even as density-based and hierarchical methods handle cases k-means cannot.

timeline.label: Lloyd (1957/1982)
timeline.description: Iterative assignment/update procedure for least-squares quantization—later known as Lloyd's algorithm.

timeline.label: MacQueen (1967)
timeline.description: Formal k-means name and convergence analysis for partitioning by cluster means.

timeline.label: Vector quantization era
timeline.description: k-means centroids used for signal compression and codebook design in engineering applications.

timeline.label: k-means++ (2007)
timeline.description: Smarter initialization reduces bad local minima; adopted as default in scikit-learn and similar tools.
